name: King's Indian Defense
description: A dynamic defense with g6 and Bg7. Black allows White's center then strikes back.

moves.commentary: Nf6 develops the knight and prepares the King's Indian setup with g6.

children.commentary: c4 stakes a claim in the center and prepares Nc3.
g6 prepares Bg7. The fianchetto is the key to the King's Indian.
Nc3 develops and supports the center.
Bg7 completes the fianchetto. The bishop eyes the long diagonal.
e4 builds a strong pawn center. The main King's Indian position.
d6 supports the center and prepares e5.
Nf3 develops the knight to its natural square.
Black castles kingside. The King's Indian proper.
Be2 develops and prepares to castle.

strategy.keyIdea: Let White build a large pawn center, then undermine and attack it from the flanks — particularly with ...e5 and a kingside pawn storm.
strategy.middleGamePlan: After ...e5, the classic plan is ...Ne8-d6 or ...Ne8-c7 to regroup, followed by ...f5 and a kingside attack. White will push on the queenside (c5, b5). This is a race — your kingside attack must come before White's queenside breakthrough.
strategy.watchOut: Playing passively and letting White consolidate — the King's Indian only works if you actively create a kingside attack.
typicalGoals: Play ...e5 to stake a claim in the center
Reroute the knight toward the kingside (Ne8-d6 or Ne8-f6-g4)
Launch a kingside pawn storm with ...f5-f4
Keep the d6 pawn solid to avoid weaknesses in your structure

quiz.question: You're playing the King's Indian. White has a strong pawn center on d4 and e4. What's your plan?

options.text: Exchange pawns in the center to simplify the position
options.explanation: Trading pawns relieves White's tension and leads to a simpler position that favors White's space advantage. The King's Indian requires active counterplay.

options.text: Attack the center with ...e5, then launch a kingside storm
options.explanation: Correct. ...e5 is the key King's Indian move — it challenges White's center and prepares the knight reroute and f5-f4 kingside push.

options.text: Expand on the queenside with ...a5-a4 to counter White's plans
options.explanation: Queenside expansion is White's plan in the King's Indian. Black's counterplay belongs on the kingside — ...e5 followed by f5-f4.

default.opponentMoveSummary: Your opponent stepped off the main theory line with an unusual move, choosing their own path rather than the expected response.
default.whyBookBreaks: The standard line assumes a particular pawn or piece configuration that this move bypasses.
default.opponentStrategy: Your opponent is trying to reach a less-charted position where their preparation or intuition gives them an edge.
forwardGuidance: Prioritize completing your development — get all minor pieces off the back rank.
Control the center with pawns or piece pressure before launching any flank operations.

f3.opponentMoveSummary: White played f3, preparing e4 and a kingside expansion with the Sämisch setup.
f3.whyBookBreaks: The King's Indian main lines assume White plays d4, c4, and Nc3. f3 signals a different plan — space and kingside attack.
f3.opponentStrategy: Sämisch Variation intent — White aims for e4, f3, and a space advantage, then launches a kingside attack.
forwardGuidance: Complete your development; White's f3 weakens the dark squares — consider how to exploit that.
Prepare ...e5 and ...f5; your counterplay belongs on the kingside.

Bg5.opponentMoveSummary: White played Bg5, pinning your king's knight and restricting your options.
Bg5.whyBookBreaks: The main line assumes White develops with Nc3 and Nf3. Bg5 pins the knight and takes away your ...Nf6-e4 resource.
Bg5.opponentStrategy: Averbakh Variation — White pins the knight to limit Black's flexibility and control key squares.
forwardGuidance: Consider ...h6 to challenge the bishop or ...Nbd7 to break the pin.
Complete development; the pin is annoying but can often be resolved.

g3.opponentMoveSummary: White played g3, fianchettoing the bishop and aiming for a symmetrical structure.
g3.whyBookBreaks: The King's Indian main lines assume White plays c4 and Nc3. g3 chooses a fianchetto system that counters your setup.
g3.opponentStrategy: Fianchetto System — White develops the bishop to g2 and aims for a solid, symmetrical position that neutralizes Black's kingside plans.
forwardGuidance: Adjust your plan; the fianchetto often leads to a slower, more positional game.
Consider ...c5 or ...e5 to create counterplay; White's setup is solid but not aggressive.
